data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-16T01:28:23.698Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically around synchronous LINQ operations.

This is a knowledge question about .NET versioning. Let me think about what I know:

In EF Core with the Cosmos DB provider, there has been a progression toward removing synchronous API support:

- In EF Core 9 (.NET 9), synchronous operations on the Cosmos DB provider were deprecated and would throw by default, but you could opt in to sync-over-async behavior.
- The opt-in was via `EnableSyncOverAsync()` or a similar configuration.


- The trajectory has been moving toward completely removing sync support, so in .NET 11, that opt-in is likely no longer available—synchronous LINQ operations on the Cosmos DB provider would no longer be supported at all.
- Though I should note that .NET 11 hasn't been released yet (it's May 2026, so November 2026 would be the release), so this is based on announced plans or preview behavior.

Let me ground this in what I know for certain: EF Core 9 with .NET 9 (November 2024) is when the Cosmos DB provider removed synchronous I/O support by default, throwing `InvalidOperationException` on sync methods unless you used an opt-in flag. From there, the trajectory would be that EF Core 10 keeps the opt-in but marks it as deprecated, and EF Core 11 removes the opt-in entirely along with the sync APIs for Cosmos.

So by .NET 11, there's no workaround—all synchronous operations on Cosmos DB would either throw or fail to compile, forcing migration to async equivalents like `ToListAsync()`, `FirstAsync()`, and `SaveChangesAsync()`. I'm sketching out what a refactored repository class would look like, converting methods to async Task-based signatures.

Writing repository methods...
